topic: Every printed family photograph in my parents' house now shows a fourth child standing next to me and my birth certificate has been legally amended to list four children
title: Every Family Photo in My Parents' House Now Has a Fourth Child. My Birth Certificate Was Amended Last Year. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I went home for my mother's seventieth birthday last month. The photographs on the mantel above the fireplace showed four children in every frame. My parents had three. I am the oldest. I have one younger brother and one younger sister. I had been home four months earlier for Easter. There had been three children in every photograph then. The same brass frames. The same Christmas trees in the background. A fourth boy my age had been inserted into every print.
description:
I went home for my mother's seventieth birthday last month. The photographs on the mantel above the fireplace showed four children in every frame. My parents had three. I am the oldest. I have one younger brother and one younger sister. I had been home four months earlier for Easter. There had been three children in every photograph then. The same brass frames. The same Christmas trees in the background. A fourth boy my age had been inserted into every print.

I pulled the family album from the closet. Every Polaroid and Christmas card going back to nineteen eighty five had been altered to include the boy. The paper was original. The Kodak watermarks on the back matched the year. I drove to the county records office on Monday and requested my own birth certificate. The certificate had been amended in August of two thousand twenty five to list me as one of four children. The fourth name was Kevin Albert. My mother had signed the amendment in person.

There is a detail about my own dining room that I have not told my brother yet. When I drove home that night there were four place settings on the table. My wife was setting the fourth as I walked in. Watch to the end. She told me Kevin would not stay long.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I went home for my mother's seventieth birthday on the first Saturday in May. I arrived at the house in the late afternoon and let myself in through the side door the way I always have. My mother was in the kitchen with my sister. The mantel above the fireplace held nine framed photographs the way it always has. The frames were the same. The order was the same. I stopped in the living room before I said hello. In every single photograph on the mantel there were four children. My parents had three. I am the oldest. I have one younger brother and one younger sister. There has never been a fourth child in our family. The fourth child in every photograph was a boy my age standing next to me.

sections.narration: The house is on Elder Lane in a quiet subdivision built in nineteen seventy two. My parents have lived in it since nineteen eighty four. It is a yellow two story colonial with black shutters and a brick chimney along the east wall. The living room is on the right when you come in. The fireplace is the centerpiece of that room. My mother curated the mantel beginning the year they moved in. Every year she added one new framed family photograph and retired one of the oldest. The arrangement has been nine frames for as long as I can remember. Christmas portraits in the center. Birthday photographs at the edges. The frames are brass with cardboard mats. She has not replaced a frame since nineteen eighty nine. The order has not changed in three decades.

sections.narration: I had been home four months earlier for Easter. I drove down on the Friday before the holiday and stayed through Monday morning. My mother had hosted twelve people for Easter dinner. She had taken photographs all weekend with the small Canon she has owned since nineteen ninety eight. There had been three children in every photograph on the mantel that weekend. I had stood in front of the mantel on the Sunday morning before church and looked at every frame. My mother sent me her Easter pictures the following Tuesday in a single email with twenty four attachments. I had downloaded all of them to my phone and never deleted them. They were still on my phone in May when I walked into the living room and counted four children on the mantel.

sections.narration: I lifted each photograph off the mantel and studied it in the afternoon light. The brass frames were the same brass frames I had grown up with. The cardboard mats had the same cream color and the same beveled edges. The same Christmas trees stood in the same corners of the same living room across thirty years of holiday portraits. The wallpaper behind us was the same. The carpet was the same. The fading along the edges of the prints was the same fading I remembered. But a fourth child had been inserted into every single photograph. He stood next to me in every frame. He had a haircut identical to mine in nineteen eighty nine. He wore clothes from the same decade as the rest of us. He was always on my right.
image_prompts: close detail of a brass framed family photograph held in two hands against a soft afternoon window light, no faces clearly visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: My first thought was that my mother had hired a local artist to update the prints as a gift for her seventieth birthday. She had done strange small art projects before. She had once commissioned a watercolor of the house. My second thought was that I was misremembering. I had not lived in the house for twenty years. Memory rewrites itself the way old paper yellows. My third thought was that I had forgotten a cousin who used to stay with us during the summers in the nineteen eighties. There was a second cousin on my father's side who had visited often. Maybe he had been in the photographs all along and I had simply edited him out of my own memory. I sat down on the couch and tried to remember a cousin named anything.

sections.narration: My brother arrived at the house an hour later with his wife and their dogs. I waited until he had set his bag down. I asked him to come into the living room and look at the mantel with me. He stood in front of the photographs and counted them. He counted the children in the center Christmas portrait. He said the number four out loud. He said it without any surprise. He said the name Kevin the way you say the name of a person you have always known. He told me Kevin had been four months younger than me. He told me Kevin had always been on my right in family pictures because he had been the shorter twin. He told me I was acting strange and asked if I had eaten anything on the drive down.

sections.narration: I waited until my parents went to bed that night and then I went to the hallway closet on the second floor. The family album had been on the second shelf from the top since I was a child. It was a heavy three ring binder with a leather cover my mother had bought at a craft store in nineteen eighty four. I carried it down to the kitchen table and opened it under the single overhead light. Every print, every Polaroid, every yearly Christmas card going back to nineteen eighty five had been altered to include the boy. The photos were not reprints. The paper was original. The fading along the edges was original. The Kodak watermarks on the back of each print matched the year the photograph had been taken. The boy was always on my right.
image_prompts: wide interior of a darkened upstairs hallway with a closet door slightly open and a soft light from inside, no figures, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I drove to the county records office on Monday morning before I left town. The office was in a brick building on Court Street that had been a bank in the nineteen forties. I filled out the request form for a certified copy of my own birth certificate. The clerk printed it on watermarked paper while I waited. I sat in my car in the parking lot and read it. The certificate had been amended in August of two thousand twenty five. The amendment was stamped and notarized on the back of the certificate. The amendment listed me as one of four children born to my parents. The fourth name on the amendment was Kevin Albert. The amendment had been requested in person by my mother at the same county office. She had signed it on the line marked parent.

sections.narration: I drove back to the house on Elder Lane that afternoon. My mother was in her reading chair beside the window with a mug of tea on the side table. I sat down across from her and asked her who Kevin was. She looked up at me over her glasses the way she does when I ask her a question she thinks I should already know the answer to. She told me he was my twin. She told me he had died of meningitis when we were three. She told me we had always celebrated his birthday on the same day as mine. She pointed to a small framed photograph beside her reading lamp. The photograph was of me at seven years old standing on the back porch. Standing next to me, holding my hand, was a boy with my face.

sections.narration: I drove home that night and arrived at my own house just after ten. The porch light was on. The kitchen light was on. I came in through the garage door and set my keys on the counter. The dining room was on my left through the open archway. There were four place settings on my own dining room table. Four plates. Four water glasses. Four napkins folded in identical small triangles. My wife was setting the fourth napkin as I walked in. She looked up at me and smiled the way she does when I come home late. She told me Kevin would not stay long. She asked me if I had remembered to wash the spare guest sheets before I left for the weekend. She seemed honestly confused that I would ask her who she meant.
